exercises.prompt: How does a traditional image discriminator evaluate an input image?

options.text: It only looks at the absolute center pixel
It scores the entire image as a single real/fake choice
It ignores the image size completely

exercises.explain: Traditional discriminators look at the entire image to output a single score, but they often miss the fine textures.

cards.body: Imagine drawing a beautiful forest, but the leaves look like blurry green blobs. Standard AI judges only look at the whole picture and say, 'Looks like a forest!' and ignore the blurry leaves. How do we force the AI to fix the details?
cards.title: The Blurry Details Problem

cards.body: A PatchGAN discriminator is a special neural network that judges an image by dividing it into a grid of small local patches (like N x N blocks) and deciding if each individual patch looks real or fake.
cards.title: What is PatchGAN?
The Magnifying Glass Inspector
cards.body: Think of an art inspector using a magnifying glass. Instead of just glancing at a massive painting from far away, they inspect small 10cm x 10cm squares one by one. If even one square has fake brushstrokes, the painting fails!

cards.title: Zooming in on a Shoe
cards.body: In Pix2Pix, PatchGAN might look at a generated 256x256 image of a shoe using 70x70 patches. It scores each patch separately, forcing the generator to make the leather texture, laces, and stitching look incredibly sharp.

cards.title: Is it a Brand New Network?
cards.body: Some think PatchGAN is a completely different architecture from a standard CNN. In reality, it is just a regular Convolutional Network! The trick is that its final output is a grid of values (like 30x30) instead of a single number.

left.text: Looks at the global layout, leading to blurry details and flat textures.

right.text: Inspects local N x N patches, forcing sharp details, hair, and textures.

exercises.prompt: Why does PatchGAN evaluate images in local N x N patches?
exercises.explain: By looking at local N x N patches, PatchGAN forces the generator to focus on high-frequency details like textures and sharp edges.

options.text: It focuses on high-frequency local textures and sharp details.
It makes the entire image output uniform in color.
It ignores local textures to look only at the general shape.

exercises.explain: True. PatchGAN classifies each patch in the grid as real or fake, rather than producing one single score for the entire image.
exercises.prompt: True or False: PatchGAN outputs a grid of real/fake decisions rather than a single global real/fake score.

exercises.sentence: PatchGAN divides the input image into N x N [blank] to evaluate local realistic details.
exercises.explain: A PatchGAN discriminator divides the image into local N x N patches and determines the authenticity of each patch.
exercises.prompt: PatchGAN divides the input image into N x N [blank] to evaluate local realistic details.

options.text: An image generator that produces realistic global layouts but has very blurry textures and smooth skin.
A generator producing realistic individual hair strands, pores, and sharp fabric textures due to patch-level evaluation.

exercises.explain: Option B exhibits the sharp local textures (hair, skin details) characteristic of PatchGAN's patch-level supervision, whereas Option A has blurry details.
exercises.prompt: Which scenario describes the effect of training a generator with a PatchGAN discriminator?

options.text: It requires the generator to process images one single pixel at a time.
It has fewer parameters, runs faster, and can be applied to arbitrarily large images.
It guarantees that the global colors are always mathematically perfect.

exercises.explain: PatchGAN uses fewer parameters because the receptive field is restricted to local N x N patches, making it faster and easier to train on different image sizes. Next, we will explore cycle consistency in unsupervised translation!
exercises.prompt: What is an additional advantage of using a PatchGAN discriminator over a global one?

concept: The PatchGAN discriminator classifies local N x N patches as real or fake, rather than the whole image, forcing the generator to produce sharp, realistic high-frequency detail.
title: The PatchGAN Discriminator
